name: Arecanut
crop_cycle_note: Perennial palm; first bearing in 5-6 years, economically productive for 40-60 years

growth_stages.name: Nursery / Seedling
care_focus: Protect from direct sun scorch and waterlogging

growth_stages.name: Juvenile / Vegetative
characteristics: Rapid leaf addition, trunk girth increasing but no visible ringed trunk yet
care_focus: Regular basin irrigation, especially in summer
Apply organic manure and shade for intercrops if any (banana/pepper)

growth_stages.name: Flowering (Inflorescence Emergence)
characteristics: Ringed trunk visible with clear leaf scars
care_focus: Increase potash application to support flowering
Monitor for spindle bug damage on the emerging inflorescence

growth_stages.name: Fruit Set
characteristics: Small green nuts forming in bunches after pollination
High vulnerability to fruit rot in this stage
care_focus: Prophylactic Bordeaux mixture spray before monsoon to prevent koleroga (fruit rot)
Avoid water stress that causes fruit drop

growth_stages.name: Fruit Maturation
characteristics: Nuts enlarge, husk color transitions from green towards yellow-orange
care_focus: Sustain irrigation for uniform nut development

growth_stages.name: Harvest
characteristics: Nuts fully colored (orange/red depending on variety), ready for processing
Bunches harvested for red or white arecanut processing depending on maturity
care_focus: Timely harvesting to match intended processing (tender/ripe)
Proper drying and storage to prevent post-harvest mold

operations: Basin irrigation 2-3 times a week depending on soil type
Apply mulch (arecanut leaves/husk) to conserve moisture
Complete Bordeaux mixture spraying before monsoon onset for fruit rot prevention
precautions: Heat stress can affect flowering quality - avoid irrigation gaps
Watch for spindle bug buildup in dry heat

operations: Ensure proper drainage in garden to avoid waterlogging
Repeat Bordeaux mixture spray after heavy showers if washed off
Inspect bunches regularly for koleroga (fruit rot) symptoms
precautions: Peak risk period for koleroga and bud rot due to continuous humidity
Avoid unnecessary basal fertilizer application in waterlogged soil

operations: Apply fertilizer split dose once soil moisture stabilizes
Begin harvest of early maturing bunches
precautions: Residual moisture still supports fungal disease - keep monitoring

operations: Main harvest season for most varieties
Reduce irrigation frequency, monitor soil moisture regularly
Post-harvest processing and drying of nuts
precautions: Ensure proper drying to prevent mold during storage

diseases.name: Koleroga (Fruit Rot / Mahali Disease)
symptoms: Water-soaked patches on young nuts turning dark brown to black
Affected nuts shrivel and fall prematurely
Rapid spread through the bunch in humid weather
treatment: Spray 1% Bordeaux mixture immediately on all bunches and crown
Remove and destroy fallen and infected nuts to reduce inoculum
Repeat spray after every heavy rain spell during monsoon
prevention: Prophylactic Bordeaux spray before monsoon onset (May-June)
Ensure good garden drainage and avoid overcrowded planting

diseases.name: Bud Rot
symptoms: Foul-smelling rot in the innermost unopened leaf (spindle)
Youngest leaves wilt and can be pulled out with ease
Palm may die if crown is destroyed
treatment: Remove all rotten tissue and apply Bordeaux paste to the wound
Drench crown region with copper oxychloride solution
Cover crown to protect from further rain entry during treatment
prevention: Avoid injury to the crown region during harvesting operations

diseases.name: Stem Bleeding
symptoms: Dark reddish-brown fluid oozing from trunk cracks
Internal tissue discoloration and gradual weakening of the trunk
treatment: Scrape affected bark and apply Bordeaux paste
Apply systemic fungicide drench near the base if severe
prevention: Avoid mechanical injury to the trunk

diseases.name: Yellow Leaf Disease (YLD)
symptoms: Uniform yellowing of leaflets, most visible on older fronds first
Reduced leaf size and canopy density over successive years
Gradual decline in nut yield and quality
treatment: No direct cure; supportive nutrition with micronutrient mixtures
Remove severely declined, unproductive palms to limit spread
prevention: Use disease-free planting material from certified nurseries
Maintain good soil health with organic matter

diseases.name: Anabe Roga (Collar Rot)
symptoms: Yellowing and drooping of lower leaves
Rotting visible at the collar/root zone when soil is dug around the base
treatment: Drench collar region with a copper fungicide solution
Improve drainage to reduce root zone moisture
prevention: Apply well-decomposed organic manure, not fresh

pests.name: Arecanut Spindle Bug
symptoms: Malformed, withered inflorescence spindle
Brown streaks on the emerging spindle before it opens
management: Spray malathion or a recommended contact insecticide on the spindle at early emergence
Reduce overcrowding and shade for better air circulation

pests.name: Inflorescence Caterpillar
symptoms: Webbing on the inflorescence with feeding damage on flowers
Premature nut/flower drop
management: Remove and destroy webbed inflorescence parts
Spray recommended insecticide during early flowering if infestation noticed

pests.name: White Grub (Root Grub)
symptoms: Yellowing and stunted growth of young palms
Grubs visible in soil when root zone is dug
management: Apply neem cake to the basin to deter grub buildup
Soil drench with a recommended systemic insecticide in severe cases

pests.name: Mealybug
symptoms: White waxy colonies on nuts and bunch stalks
Sooty mold development from honeydew secretion
management: Spray neem oil (2%) directly on bunch clusters
Control ant movement on the trunk which protects mealybugs

risk_conditions.impact: Sharp rise in koleroga (fruit rot) and bud rot incidence
Flower and nut drop due to moisture stress
Reduced pollination success and spindle bug buildup
Bunch and frond damage, increased fruit drop
